preamble: The shift from the bilabial stop [b] to the labiodental (or bilabial) fricative [v] or approximant [β] is a classic instance of spirantization (lenition). It is especially common in intervocalic position, where the oral closure weakens. In Spanish, this merged with the reflex of Latin /v/ (originally [w]) to produce a single phoneme realized as [b] word-initially and [β] elsewhere.

examples.note: Begedkefat spirantization: post-vocalic /b/ → [v] in Biblical Hebrew. E.g., כָּתַב 'katav' (he wrote).
Intervocalic Latin /b/ spirantized to [β]; also illustrates betacism (merger of /b/ and /v/).

Intervocalic lenition in Middle Persian: /b/ after a vowel shifted to the approximant [w] (sometimes transcribed as /v/). The process began in the Arsacid period and was fully established in Sassanid-era Middle Persian (3rd–7th c. CE).
Intervocalic /b/ → /w/ ~ /v/; the word shows the characteristic Iranian weakening of voiced stops between vowels.

Sogdian underwent spirantization of voiced stops more radically than Persian: /b/ → [β] in all positions including word-initially (not just intervocalically). This Eastern Iranian feature distinguished Sogdian from Middle Persian.

Kabyle spirantization: lax /b/ in intervocalic position weakens to the voiced bilabial fricative [β], approaching [v] in some realizations. Geminate /bb/ and post-nasal /mb/ resist spirantization and surface as stops.
Northern Berber spirantization rule: lax (non-geminate) /b/ between vowels or sonorants → [β]. Absent in Zenaga and Tetserret (non-spirantizing Berber branches).

sources: Skjærvø, P. O. (2009). Middle Persian. In Windfuhr (ed.), The Iranian Languages. Routledge.
Kossmann, M. G. & Stroomer, H. J. (1997). Berber Phonology. In Kaye (ed.), Phonologies of Asia and Africa. Eisenbrauns.
allophones_note: /b/ surfaces as [v] or [β] after a vowel; [b] is preserved word-initially and after consonants. Documented in Biblical Hebrew (Beged-Kefet), Spanish, and Berber.
period: Vulgar Latin period / Middle Iranian period
